title: How K-Means Actually Groups Data
concept: K-Means groups data by placing K centroids and assigning every point to whichever centroid is closest, turning "find hidden groups" into a concrete distance calculation.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Clustering groups data points based on what property?

options.text: How similar they are to each other
The order they were collected in
A label a human assigned in advance

exercises.explain: Right! Clustering groups points by similarity, not by pre-set labels.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Clustering algorithms need to be told the correct group for each data point before they run.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Clustering discovers the groups itself — no correct answers are given ahead of time.

cards.title: Picking meeting spots for four friend groups
cards.body: Imagine dropping four flags on a map, one for each friend group, then telling everyone to walk to whichever flag is closest to them. That's basically what K-Means does — except the "flags" and the "walking" happen with data points instead of people.

cards.title: What is K-Means?
cards.body: K-Means is a clustering algorithm that picks K center points, called centroids, and assigns every data point to whichever centroid it is closest to. Each centroid plus the points assigned to it forms one cluster.

cards.title: Cafeteria tables, not seating charts
cards.body: Think of K centroids as K empty cafeteria tables placed around a room. Every student then sits at whichever table is nearest to them. Nobody assigns seats — proximity to a table decides the group, and K-Means works the same way with numbers instead of students.

cards.title: Closest by distance, not by feeling
cards.body: Say a centroid sits at coordinates (2, 3) and a data point sits at (2.5, 3.1). K-Means measures the straight-line distance between them and compares it to the point's distance from every other centroid. Whichever centroid is numerically nearest wins that point — every single time.

cards.title: The centroid isn't a real data point
cards.body: You might assume a centroid must be one of the actual data points, like a chosen "representative" customer. In reality, a centroid is usually a calculated average position — it can land somewhere no real data point ever touched, as you'll see when the algorithm updates it.

cards.title: Centroid vs. data point
cards.body: Two very different roles in the same picture:

left.text:
A center position
Represents one whole cluster

right.text:
One real observation
Assigned to its nearest centroid

exercises.prompt: In K-Means, what determines which cluster a data point belongs to?

options.text: Which centroid is closest to that point
The order the point appears in the dataset
A label the point was given before clustering started
How many other points are in the dataset

exercises.explain: Correct! Every point is assigned to whichever centroid it is nearest to.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The 'K' in K-Means refers to the number of centroids (clusters) the algorithm will create.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! K is simply the number of clusters you want the algorithm to form.

exercises.sentence: K-Means assigns each data point to its closest ___.
exercises.explain: Each point joins the cluster of whichever centroid is nearest to it.

exercises.prompt: A weather app groups 500 cities into 5 climate zones by placing 5 center points and assigning each city to its nearest one. Which technique is this?

options.text: K-Means clustering
Linear regression
Manual labeling

exercises.explain: Right! Placing center points and assigning items to the nearest one is exactly how K-Means works.

exercises.prompt: A centroid sits at position (0, 0). Point A is at (1, 1) and Point B is at (5, 5). If these are the only two centroids being compared against, which point is closer to the centroid at (0,0)?

options.text: Point A, because it has a smaller distance to (0,0)
Point B, because it has larger coordinates
They are exactly the same distance away
Distance cannot be compared between points

exercises.explain: Exactly! (1,1) is much closer to the origin than (5,5) is, so Point A would be assigned to that centroid.

exercises.prompt: A streaming service wants to sort 1 million users into 6 taste groups. They place 6 centroids in the data and assign each user to the nearest one. A colleague asks: "But how did they decide which users go in group 3 versus group 4?" What's the correct answer?

options.text: Purely by distance — each user was assigned to whichever of the 6 centroids was numerically closest to them
A human manually reviewed each of the 1 million users and picked a group
Users were split alphabetically by username into 6 equal groups
The order users signed up determined their group

exercises.explain: Exactly! K-Means assignment is purely distance-based — no manual sorting, no alphabetical tricks, just nearest centroid wins. Next: how those centroids actually get placed and refined!
